perturbation.original_text: The Blockchain Administrator agrees to maintain and keep all books, accounts and other records of the Fund that relate to activities performed by the Blockchain Administrator hereunder and will maintain and keep such books, accounts and records in accordance with the Investment Company Act. In compliance with the requirements of Rule 31a-3 under the Investment Company Act, the Blockchain Administrator agrees that all records which it maintains for the Fund shall at all times remain the property of the Fund, shall be readily accessible during normal business hours, and shall be promptly surrendered upon the termination of the Agreement or otherwise on written request. The Blockchain Administrator further agrees that all records which it maintains for the Fund pursuant to Rule 31a-1 under the Investment Company Act will be preserved for the periods prescribed by Rule 31a-2 under the Investment Company Act unless any such records are earlier surrendered as provided above. Records shall be surrendered in usable machine-readable form. The Blockchain Administrator shall have the right to retain copies of such records subject to observance of its confidentiality obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Blockchain Administrator agrees to maintain and keep some books, accounts and other records of the Fund that relate to activities performed by the Blockchain Administrator hereunder. Records shall be surrendered as is. The Blockchain Administrator shall have the right to retain copies of such records subject to observance of its confidentiality obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change omits the requirement to maintain records in accordance with the Investment Company Act and the specific rules 31a-1, 31a-2, and 31a-3, and significantly weakens the requirements for accessibility and surrender of records, violating the Investment Company Act's record-keeping requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940, Rule 31a-1, 31a-2, and 31a-3
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text explicitly states compliance with Rule 31a-1, 31a-2 and 31a-3 under the Investment Company Act which mandates specific record-keeping practices, accessibility, and preservation requirements for investment companies. The modified text removes these specific compliance requirements, making the contract non-compliant with these SEC rules.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 270.31a-1 - Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 270.31a-1 - Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 270.31a-1 Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. (a) Every registered investment company, and every underwriter , broker, dealer, or investment adviser which is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and keep current the accounts, books, and other documents relating to its business which constitute the record forming the basis for financial statements required to be filed pursuant to section 30 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and of the auditor's certificates relating thereto. (b) Every registered investment company shall maintain and keep current the following books, accounts, and other documents: (1) Journals (or other records of original entry) containing an itemized daily record in detail of all purchases and sales of securities (including sales and redemptions of its own securities), all receipts and deliveries of securities (including certificate numbers if such detail is not recorded by custodian or transfer agent), all receipts and disbursements of cash and all other debits and credits. Such records shall show for each such transaction the name and quantity of securities, the unit and aggregate purchase or sale price, commission paid, the market on which effected, the trade date, the settlement date, and the name
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits critical requirements mandated by the Investment Company Act, specifically concerning record-keeping. The original text explicitly states compliance with rules 31a-1, 31a-2, and 31a-3, emphasizing specific record-keeping practices, accessibility, and preservation. The changed text removes these compliance requirements and weakens the accessibility of records, directly conflicting with the legal obligations described in 17 CFR § 270.31a-1, which mandates that registered investment companies maintain and keep current the accounts, books, and other documents relating to its business. This omission constitutes a clear non-compliance, justifying the 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the preceding sentence of this Section 5 to the contrary, nothing contained herein shall protect or be deemed to protect the Indemnified Parties against or entitle or be deemed to entitle the Indemnified Parties to indemnification in respect of, any liability to the Fund or its security holders to which the Indemnified Parties would otherwise be subject by reason of criminal conduct, willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence in the performance of the Blockchain Administrator's duties or by reason of the reckless disregard of the Blockchain Administrator's duties and obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the preceding sentence of this Section 5 to the contrary, nothing contained herein shall protect or be deemed to protect the Indemnified Parties against or entitle or be deemed to entitle the Indemnified Parties to indemnification.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit exceptions to the indemnification clause, specifically the part stating that the indemnified parties are not protected from liability due to criminal conduct, willful misfeasance, bad faith, gross negligence, or reckless disregard of duties. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it could allow indemnification for actions that are legally non-indemnifiable under the Investment Company Act of 1940 and general principles of corporate law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act prohibits exculpation of officers and directors from liability for conduct constituting breach of fiduciary duty. The original clause correctly excluded indemnification for criminal conduct, willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence. Removing this exception allows for indemnification in cases where it is legally prohibited, contradicting the Investment Company Act.

Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title),
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 - Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-17 - Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title), or any affiliated person of such a person, promoter, or principal underwriter, acting as principal— (1) knowingly to sell any security or other property to such registered company or to any company controlled by such registered company , unless such sale involves solely (A) securities of which the buyer is the issuer, (B) securities of which the seller is the issuer and which are part of a general offering to the holders of a class of its securities, or (C) securities deposited with the trustee of a unit investment trust or periodic payment plan by the depositor thereof; (2) knowingly to purchase from such registered company , or from any company controlled by such registered company , any security or other property (except securities of which the seller is the issuer) ; (3) to borrow money or other property from such registered company or from any company controlled by such registered company (unless the borrower is controlled by the lender) except as permitted in section 80a–21(b) of this title ; or (4) to loan money or other property to such registered company , or to any company controlled by such registered company , in contravention of such rules, regulations, or orders as the Commission may, after consultation with and taking into consideration the views of the Federal banking agencies (as defined in section 1813 of title 12 ), prescribe or issue consistent w
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits critical exceptions to indemnification, specifically removing the clause that denies protection for liabilities arising from criminal conduct, willful misfeasance, bad faith, gross negligence, or reckless disregard of duties. This omission contradicts the principle that officers and directors should not be exculpated from liability for breaches of fiduciary duty, as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act. While the snippets do not explicitly state the prohibition of indemnification for such conduct, the Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight the legal implication that removing these exceptions allows for indemnification where it is legally prohibited, thus undermining the original clause's compliance with fiduciary standards. The omission creates a loophole, potentially allowing indemnification for actions that are legally non-indemnifiable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts formed and to be performed entirely within the State of New York, without regard to conflict of laws principles, and in accordance with the applicable provisions of the Investment Fund Act. In such case, to the extent the applicable laws of the State of New York, or any of the provisions herein, conflict with the provisions of the Investment Fund Act, the latter shall control.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts formed and to be performed entirely within the State of New York, without regard to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the clause stating that the agreement shall be construed in accordance with the applicable provisions of the Investment Fund Act and that the Investment Fund Act will control in case of conflict with New York Law. This creates a legal contradiction because it implies that New York state law would supersede federal law, which is not permissible under the Supremacy Clause of the US Constitution, especially concerning registered investment companies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause, Article VI of the U.S. Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution establishes that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land, taking precedence over state laws. By removing the acknowledgement that the Investment Fund Act (a federal law) controls in case of conflict, the contract now implies that New York law could potentially override federal law in matters concerning a registered investment company. This is a direct contradiction to the Supremacy Clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit acknowledgment that the Investment Fund Act controls in case of conflict with New York law. This omission contradicts the Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, as federal law takes precedence over state law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states: "This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof...shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding." By removing the clause that the Investment Fund Act controls, the contract implies that New York law could supersede federal law, directly contradicting this principle of federal supremacy. This omission makes the agreement non-compliant.
